corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Performance varies substantially from run to run (with different random initializations of the policy), but we could not obtain error statistics due to time constraints. made available through the Arcade Learning Environment (Bellemare et al., 2013) The images were preprocessed fol- lowing the protocol in Mnih et al (2013), and the policy was represented by the convolutional neural network shown in Figure 3, with two convolutional layers with 16 channels and stride 2 , followed by one fully-connected layer with 20 units, yielding 33,500 parameters.

location.sentence: By making several approximations to the theoretically-justified procedure, we develop a practical algorithm, called Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO).

context_window.previous: We describe an iterative procedure for optimizing policies, with guaranteed monotonic improve- ment.
context_window.current: By making several approximations to the theoretically-justified procedure, we develop a practical algorithm, called Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO).
context_window.next: This algorithm is similar to natural policy gradient methods and is effec- tive for optimizing large nonlinear policies such as neural networks.

location.sentence: (55) Trust Region Policy Optimization The method we will describe involves two steps: (1) compute a search direction, using a linear approximation to objective and quadratic approximation to the constraint; and (2) perform a line search in that direction, ensuring that we improve the nonlinear objective while satisfying the nonlinear constraint.

context_window.previous: That gives ‖ G ∆ ˜ Gρ ‖ 1 ≤ ‖ G ‖ 1 ‖ ∆ ‖ 1 ‖ ˜ G ‖ 1 ‖ ρ ‖ 1 = 1 1 − γ · 2 α · 1 1 − γ · 1 (53) So we have that γ 2 ∣ ∣ ∣ rG ∆ G ∆ ˜ Gρ ∣ ∣ ∣ ≤ γ ‖ γrG ∆ ‖ ∞ ‖ G ∆ ˜ Gρ ‖ 1 ≤ γ ‖ v ∆ ‖ ∞ ‖ G ∆ ˜ Gρ ‖ 1 ≤ γ · 2 α · 2 α (1 − γ ) 2 = 4 γ (1 − γ ) 2 α 2 (54) C Efficiently Solving the Trust-Region Constrained Optimization Problem This section describes how to efficiently approximately solve the following constrained optimization problem, which we must solve at each iteration of TRPO: maximize L ( θ ) subject to D KL ( θ old , θ ) ≤ δ.
context_window.current: (55) Trust Region Policy Optimization The method we will describe involves two steps: (1) compute a search direction, using a linear approximation to objective and quadratic approximation to the constraint; and (2) perform a line search in that direction, ensuring that we improve the nonlinear objective while satisfying the nonlinear constraint.
context_window.next: The search direction is computed by approximately solving the equation Ax = g , where A is the Fisher information matrix, i.e., the quadratic approximation to the KL divergence constraint: D KL ( θ old , θ ) ≈ 1 2 ( θ − θ old ) T A ( θ − θ old ) , where A ij = ∂ ∂θ i ∂ ∂θ j D KL ( θ old , θ ) .

location.sentence: (1983), using a linear policy with six parameters that is easy to opti- mize with derivative-free black-box optimization methods.

context_window.current: (1983), using a linear policy with six parameters that is easy to opti- mize with derivative-free black-box optimization methods.
context_window.next: The following algorithms were considered in the compari- son: single path TRPO ; vine TRPO ; cross-entropy method (CEM), a gradient-free method (Szita & L¨ orincz, 2006); covariance matrix adaption (CMA), another gradient-free method (Hansen & Ostermeier, 1996); natural gradi- ent , the classic natural policy gradient algorithm (Kakade, 2002), which differs from single path by the use of a fixed penalty coefficient (Lagrange multiplier) instead of the KL divergence constraint; empirical FIM , identical to single path , except that the FIM is estimated using the covariance matrix of the gradients rather than the analytic estimate; max KL , which was only tractable on the cart-pole problem, and uses the maximum KL divergence in Equation (11), rather than the average divergence, allowing us to evaluate the quality of this approximation.

We describe two variants of this algorithm: first, the single-path method, which can be applied in the model-free setting; second, the vine method, which requires the system to be restored to particular states, which is typically only possible in simulation.
These algorithms are scalable and can optimize nonlinear policies with tens of thousands of parameters, which have previously posed a major challenge for model-free policy search .

We proved monotonic improvement for an algorithm that repeatedly optimizes a local approximation to the expected return of the policy with a KL divergence penalty, and we showed that an approximation to this method that incorporates a KL divergence constraint achieves good empirical results on a range of challenging policy learning tasks, outperforming prior methods.

We proved monotonic improvement for an algorithm that repeatedly optimizes a local approximation to the expected return of the policy with a KL divergence penalty, and we showed that an approximation to this method that incorporates a KL divergence constraint achieves good empirical results on a range of challenging policy learning tasks, outperforming prior methods.

Our analysis also provides a perspective that unifies policy gradient and policy iteration methods, and shows them to be special limiting cases of an algorithm that optimizes a certain objective subject to a trust region constraint.
